title: Failure mode = what it looks like., Reliability mechanism = why it happens, Root cause = where it started.
author: M
text:
M

moh.kolb
Member

Failure modes are what the failure looks like.

Reliability mechanisms explain why it happens.

Root cause is found through correlation.

In semiconductor packaging, we often describe failures by their visible form:

wire bond lift-off,
solder voids,
die cracking,
delamination,
warpage,
corrosion,
opens,
leakage,
intermittent failures.

These are important observations, but they are usually symptoms.

The deeper question is:

What physical mechanism created the failure?

High-temperature storage may expose diffusion, intermetallic growth, and Kirkendall voiding.

Temperature-humidity-bias or HAST may expose moisture ingress, ionic contamination, electrochemical corrosion, bias sensitivity, and weak material interfaces.

Thermal cycling may expose CTE mismatch, fatigue, delamination, solder-joint stress, and warpage.

The visible failure tells us what happened.

The reliability mechanism helps explain why it happened.

But root cause requires correlation across design, materials, wafer process, package architecture, OSAT assembly, inspection, electrical test, qualification, and field behavior.

A solder void may not be only a solder problem.

A bond failure may not be only a wire-bond problem.

An optical drift issue in CPO may not be only a photonics problem.

Advanced packaging needs more than defect detection.

It needs root-cause evidence.

Failure modes show the symptom.

Reliability mechanisms reveal the physics.

Correlation finds the root cause.
